title: NOC 65101 – Service station attendants | GTR Immigration
description: Service station attendants under NOC 65101 are the operational heartbeat of Canada’s vast transportation and energy distribution network. As a TEER 5 occupation, this role offers a highly accessible entry point for international workers into the Canadian labor market. Whether working in bustling urban centers or along the critical Trans-Canada Highway corridors, these professionals provide essential refueling, maintenance, and retail services. For immigration, this role is a key component of Provincial Nominee Programs (PNPs) and the Atlantic Immigration Program (AIP), particularly in regions where logistics and transportation are primary economic drivers.

classification.teer: Classification LevelDetailsBroad Occupation Category6 – Sales and service occupationsTEER Level5Major Group65 – Sales and service support occupationsSub-Major Group651 – Sales and service support occupationsMinor Group6510 – Sales and service support occupationsNOC Code65101

duties: Refuel vehicles (cars, trucks, and buses) and watercraft with gasoline, diesel, or alternative fuels (EV, Propane, Hydrogen).
Perform basic vehicle maintenance such as checking oil levels, tire pressure, and replenishing windshield washer fluid.
Clean windshields and windows for customers.
Operate point-of-sale systems to process payments for fuel, automotive products, and convenience store items.
Maintain the cleanliness of the station pumps, parking areas, and retail sections.
Monitor fuel levels in storage tanks and report discrepancies to management.
Assist in stocking shelves and maintaining inventory of automotive fluids and accessories.
employment_requirements: Some secondary school education is usually required.
On-the-job training is provided for all safety protocols and equipment operations.
Basic mathematical and customer service skills are necessary.
High proficiency in English or French (or both) is required for effective communication with customers.
Familiarity with basic vehicle mechanics is a significant asset but not mandatory.
job_outlook: The outlook for service station attendants is Favourable. Despite the shift toward electric vehicles (EVs), the infrastructure for EV charging and high-speed travel centers is growing, requiring attendants to manage complex charging bays and enhanced retail services. The retirement of the existing workforce will also create a steady stream of openings for new immigrants.
labour_market_demand: The demand for service station attendants is Very Good and remains constant year-round. While self-service stations are common, the rise of "Full-Service" luxury stations and large-scale "Travel Centers" (Truck Stops) has created a significant need for attendants to manage refueling, basic vehicle checks, and retail operations. In 2026, demand is particularly high in Ontario, British Columbia, and the Atlantic provinces, where tourism and freight transport are expanding.
High Accessibility: Start working and gaining Canadian experience immediately without the need for extensive prior training or high-level degrees.
Essential Service Stability: Enjoy job security in a sector that is vital to the nation's infrastructure and movement of goods.
Path to Management: Use this role as a stepping stone to become a Service Station Supervisor (NOC 62020) or move into specialized automotive service roles.
Regional Opportunities: This role is available in every corner of Canada, from the largest cities to the most remote northern communities.
pathways: Atlantic Immigration Program (AIP): A high-conversion route for TEER 5 workers with job offers from designated employers in the Atlantic region.
BC PNP Entry-Level and Semi-Skilled (ELSS): A dedicated pathway for those working in the tourism, hospitality, or retail sectors in British Columbia for at least 9 months.
Alberta Opportunity Stream: Fast-track PR for those currently working in Alberta on a valid work permit in eligible service sectors.
Saskatchewan INP (Hard-to-Fill Skills Pilot): Addressing critical labor shortages in the service and retail industries.
